screen.name: photoMenu
screen.title: Select the photograph you want to analyze.

button.title: V-shaped burn pattern
button.summary: Points to the space heater as possible fire origin.

button.title: Crazed glass
button.summary: Caused by the water used for dousing the fire.

button.title: Protected surface
button.summary: The victim was laying on the living room floor during the fire.

button.title: Puddle-shaped burn pattern
button.summary: May be the result of using an ignitable liquid.

button.title: Burned outlet
button.summary: The space heater was probably on when the fire started.

screen.name: vShaped1
screen.title: V-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc:
This v-shaped burn pattern is right above the burned space heater. Could this be the origin of the fire?
Open the database to search for more information.

screen.name: vShaped2
screen.title: V-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc:
Fires can produce characteristic burn patterns when they are close to a wall.
A v-shaped burn pattern like this one often develops in the area where the fire originated.

screen.name: vShaped3
screen.text: A fire starts burning with a small flame.
As the fire progresses, the fire and the smoke it produces are sent upward.
The fire continues to grow and now includes the ceiling.

screen.name: vShaped4
screen.title: A conical, v-shaped pattern results.
screen.desc: This picture shows a v-shaped pattern that was caused by a burning trash can, which later turned out to be the origin of the fire.
screen.caption: The trashcan was here

screen.name: vShaped5
screen.title: V-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc:
Fires can produce characteristic burn patterns when they are close to a wall.
A v-shaped burn pattern like this one often develops in the area where the fire originated.

screen.name: vShaped6
screen.title: V-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc: Was the space heater the origin of the fire?
screen.response: No. A v-shaped pattern often develops at the origin of fire, but not always. Try again.
I agree. You should examine the space heater further to confirm that it was the fire origin.
We can't exclude it - a v-shaped pattern often develops at the origin of the fire. Try again.

screen.name: glass1
screen.title: Crazed glass
screen.desc: First, use the slider to zoom into a crazed area.

screen.name: glass2
screen.title: Crazed glass
screen.desc: Good. Now let's get some information about it.

screen.name: glass3
screen.title: Crazed glass
screen.desc:
Crazing is a term used to describe a complicated pattern of short cracks in glass.
Lab tests revealed that crazing is the result of rapid cooling of hot glass by the application of water.
screen.popup: Glass heated in a propane flame for 5 min, cooled naturally.
Glass heated in a propane flame for 5 min, then sprayed with water.

screen.name: glass4
screen.title: Crazed glass
screen.desc: Does the crazed glass prove it was arson?
screen.response: There's no scientific evidence that crazing has anything to do with arson. Try again.
Remember, the crazing was caused by the water used to douse the fire. Try again.
Right; the crazed glass is not conclusive.

screen.name: surface1
screen.title: Protected Surface
screen.desc:
Looks like this is the outline of the victim.
Let's find out how it was formed.

screen.name: surface2
screen.title: Protected Surface
screen.desc:
Objects - and sometimes bodies - on the floor can protect the areas that they cover from charring or from soot.
Thus, protected surfaces show where these objects were during the fire.
screen.popup: Area protected from charring by boxes on the floor.

screen.name: surface3
screen.title: Protected Surface
screen.desc: What does the protected surface tell us about the victim?
screen.option: The body was placed there after the fire.
The body was laying there during the fire.
screen.response: If that was the case, we wouldn't see a body-shaped protected surface. Try again.
I agree. The victim was laying in this exact area on the living room floor during the fire.
There is a conclusion you can draw from the protected surface. Try again.

screen.name: puddle1
screen.title: Puddle-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc: It looks like the fire burned more intensely in this puddle-shaped area than in other areas.

screen.name: puddle2
screen.title: Puddle-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc:
Irregular burn patterns, such as this "puddle-shaped" pattern, often occur in areas of intense burning.
These patterns can occur with or without the use of ignitable liquids, such as gasoline.
screen.popup: Pattern produced by burning an ignitable liquid ("pour pattern").
Pattern produced by burning a cardboard box.

screen.name: puddle3
screen.title: Puddle-shaped burn pattern
screen.desc: Was this pattern caused by an ignitable liquid?
screen.response: Remember, puddle-shaped patterns can occur without using ignitable liquids. You're jumping to conclusions. Try again.
We can't exclude that it was caused by an ignitable liquid just by looking at the picture. Further tests are required. Try again.
Correct. We need to confirm the presence of ignitable liquids in the burned wood flooring with gas chromatography.

screen.name: outlet1
screen.title: Burned outlet
screen.desc: This one's easy. Do you think the space heater was running when the fire started?
screen.response: The fact that the space heater was plugged in doesn't necessarily mean it was on. Try again.
It's possible that it was on. You should confirm this by examining the space heater more closely.
We can't exclude that the space heater was on at the time the fire started. Try again.
